description: Algebra Introduction for grade 8. Extends domain/edu/pre-algebra/v1 by activating Law 3 (Interchangeability/Substitution) and Law 5 (Relational Mapping). Covers slope and rate of change, linear forms and graphing, and three methods for solving systems of two linear equations. Artifacts 6–10 of 15 in the full curriculum. Prerequisite: Pre-Algebra (domain/edu/pre-algebra/v1). Gateway to Algebra 1 (domain/edu/algebra-1/v1).

invariants.description: Law 1 (Balance Principle): both sides of an equation must remain algebraically equal after every transformation step. In systems, each individual equation must stay balanced; adding two balanced equations is valid because both sides of the sum are equivalent.
Division by zero or any other undefined operation is not permitted.
The proposed solution (x, y) must satisfy ALL original equations in the system. Verifying in only one equation is a Law 3 failure.

Law 2 (Inverse Operations): inverse operations must be applied in correct reversed-PEMDAS order. Inherited from pre-algebra; active during back-substitution. NOTE: LLM-estimated; deterministic tool_adapter support planned for v0.2.
Non-standard solution methods are acceptable but should be flagged for Domain Authority review.
Solutions must show all required phases: setup/isolate, substitution or elimination operation, single-variable solve, back-substitution, and verification in both original equations.

Law 3 (Interchangeability): an expression for a variable is a binding identity — it must be verified in ALL equations of the system, not only the one being simplified. Substituting into only one equation is the canonical Law 3 failure. NOTE: 'substitution_valid' is a new evidence field, currently estimated by the LLM interpreter; deterministic tool_adapter support is planned for v0.2.

Law 5 (Relational Mapping): in y = mx + b the slope m is Δy/Δx and b is the starting value. Inverting slope (reading y-change as x-change or swapping slope and intercept) is the canonical Law 5 failure. NOTE: 'relationship_correctly_mapped' is a new evidence field, currently LLM-estimated; deterministic tool_adapter support is planned for v0.2.
The student's challenge level has drifted outside the ZPD band in >= 30% of the recent window turns.
The student's challenge level has drifted outside the ZPD band in >= 50% of recent window turns, or for 3+ consecutive turns.
The student is solving problems correctly but consistently exceeding the time threshold, indicating procedural automaticity has not been achieved at the current tier.

standing_orders.description: Ask the student to show their work step by step. For systems: show setup, substitution or elimination operation, single-variable solve, back-substitution, and verification in each original equation.
Ask the student to substitute their solution pair (x, y) back into BOTH original equations and confirm both sides balance in each.

Prompt the student to examine which equations they have checked their solution in. Ask them to substitute the answer into the equation they have not yet verified. Do not state the interchangeability rule directly on the first prompt.

Provide the Law 5 scaffolding: ask the student to identify which quantity changes when x increases by exactly 1 unit. Ask: 'If x goes from 2 to 3, what happens to y?' Do not point out their inversion directly on the first prompt.
Provide the Law 2 scaffolding: ask the student to list the operations applied to the variable (in order), then ask them to undo those operations starting from the last one.
Acknowledge the non-standard approach and ask the student to explain their reasoning. Flag for Domain Authority review.
Reduce challenge level on the next task. Offer an optional hint. Provide additional scaffolding in the explanation.
Pause the current task. Issue one probe question to check understanding. If the probe response does not improve state, escalate to teacher.
Provide a targeted procedural hint for the specific step the student is slowing on. Do not solve the problem.

escalation_triggers.condition: A critical invariant (equivalence_preserved, no_illegal_operations, or solution_verifies) was violated and its standing order has been exhausted without resolution.
The zpd_intervene_or_escalate standing order was applied and the probe response did not reduce outside_pct below the minor threshold.
The domain-lib ZPD monitor determined that frustration or major drift warrants teacher intervention.
The standard_method_preferred invariant fired and the student could not explain their non-standard reasoning after one justification request.

artifacts.name: Slope & Rate of Change
artifacts.description: The student can calculate slope from a table, two points, or a graph as Δy/Δx, interpret it as a rate of change in context, and distinguish slope from the y-intercept starting value. Law 5 entry point.
artifacts.unlock_condition: mastery >= 0.8 on all required skills, confirmed by boss challenge

artifacts.name: Slope-Intercept Form & Graphing
artifacts.description: The student can identify slope and y-intercept from y = mx + b, graph a line by plotting (0, b) and using m as rise/run, and write the equation of a line given a point and slope. Laws 1 and 5 active together.
artifacts.unlock_condition: mastery >= 0.8 on all required skills, confirmed by boss challenge

artifacts.name: Systems of Equations — Graphing Method
artifacts.description: The student can solve a 2×2 system by graphing both lines, identifying the intersection, and verifying the coordinates in both original equations. Law 3 entry artifact.
artifacts.unlock_condition: mastery >= 0.8 on all required skills, confirmed by boss challenge

artifacts.name: Systems of Equations — Substitution Method
artifacts.description: The student can solve a 2×2 system where one variable is isolated by substituting its expression into the second equation, solving, back-substituting, and verifying in both equations. Canonical Law 3 artifact.
artifacts.unlock_condition: mastery >= 0.8 on all required skills, confirmed by boss challenge

artifacts.name: Systems of Equations — Elimination Method
artifacts.description: The student can solve a 2×2 system by creating opposing coefficients, adding/subtracting the equations to eliminate a variable, solving for the remaining variable, back-substituting, and verifying in both equations. Laws 1, 2, and 3 simultaneously active.
artifacts.unlock_condition: mastery >= 0.8 on all required skills, confirmed by boss challenge

equation_difficulty_tiers.label: Entry — Slope & Rates
equation_difficulty_tiers.equation_template: slope from table/graph  or  read slope and intercept from y = mx + b

equation_difficulty_tiers.label: Intermediate — Linear Equations in Two Variables
equation_difficulty_tiers.equation_template: y = mx + b — write equation from point+slope, convert forms, graph

equation_difficulty_tiers.label: Advanced — Systems of Two Linear Equations
equation_difficulty_tiers.equation_template: {y = ax + b, cx + dy = e}  solved by graphing, substitution, or elimination

evidence_schema.notes: New evidence fields introduced by this module: substitution_valid (bool, LLM-estimated, tool_adapter support planned for v0.2), relationship_correctly_mapped (bool, same status), context_interpretation_correct (bool, LLM-estimated — confirms student can interpret slope/intercept in real-world context). Inherited from pre-algebra: reversibility_order_correct, inequality_direction_correct, correctness, substitution_check, step_count, equivalence_preserved, illegal_operations, off_task_ratio, frustration_marker_count, hint_used.

glossary.definition: The rate of change of a line — how much y changes for every 1-unit increase in x. Calculated as Δy ÷ Δx (rise/run). In y = mx + b, slope is m.
The value of y when x = 0. In y = mx + b, the y-intercept is b. It is the starting value before x has changed.
The equation form y = mx + b where m is slope and b is y-intercept. Start graphing at (0, b) then use m as rise/run.
The form y − y₁ = m(x − x₁), used to build a line equation from one known point and slope.
Two or more equations that must ALL be satisfied by the same (x, y) pair simultaneously. Each equation is an independent constraint.
Solve a system by replacing a variable everywhere using its equivalent expression, solving the resulting single-variable equation, then verifying in ALL original equations.
Solve a system by multiplying equations so one variable's coefficients are opposites, adding to eliminate it, then solving and back-substituting.
The point where two lines meet. For a system of two linear equations, the intersection is the solution — the unique (x,y) satisfying both equations simultaneously.
After solving for one variable in a system, substitute its value into an original equation to find the other variable. Required to produce the complete solution pair (x, y).
How much one quantity changes relative to a 1-unit change in another. In algebra it is the slope: Δy/Δx. A constant rate of change means the relationship is linear.

actor_types.label: Student
actor_types.description: Learner working through algebra-intro artifacts, producing evidence via problem-solving steps.

learners.description: Actors who produce evidence by working through domain artifacts and problem-solving steps.

group_libraries.description: Turn interpretation output schema — defines the JSON structure the SLM must emit when classifying student intent.
